sentences.text: There was an underground movement in-- in Brinnlitz, and was five sets of five people.
We didn't know about it that it's five sets, or 10 sets.
We know only that the one set--
Separate set of five people.
Those were all part of Schindler's factory, yes.
And my unit were Joe [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] was a Russian boy, [NON-ENGLISH] was a Russian boy-- born in Russia-- Jews.
Which means they went to army, and then later they were, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how you say, the Germans--
Captured and they didn't-- they didn't reveal their religion and they were kept in-- in [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH], or whatever it is.
And somehow, from Budzin they came to Majdanek.
And from Majdanek they came to Schindler.
And I said, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and Victor Lewis-- this was I-- we didn't know that existed another unit.
We knew only about this one unit.
Now after the war, we got to know that Schindler knew about it.
There is an underground movement but he said nothing.
Talking about Schindler, I want to tell you he was a wonderful man.
He, as far I understand now and read about him, and are reading about him and getting to know from other people-- because I didn't know him.
I got to know him--
I got to know about Schindler when to I moved to Brinnlitz.
And this was October 1944, and the end of the year was next May.
It was a very short time.
But people were talking, praising very, very high.
He, himself, was a rich man from Sudetenland, where we were, from the same town.
And he was a spy for Hitler before 1939. 1937.
I think somewhere before 30th.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some people saying that he loves money.
He made money.
He made money on Jews.
He lived like king.
And he made money, because when he left Brinnlitz, we know that he had money.
What he did with the money, no one knows.
After the war.
When he was living in Brinnlitz, he knew that there is an underground.
He took his 10 rifles what he had.
Everyone was different because he was shooting-- I don't know, some wilderness.
Like-- like any other people.
He didn't shoot people.
This I'm sure.
But why he wasn't-- why he wasn't-- why he wasn't-- why he was so good in 1942, just three years before?
He took 1,000 jewels and he created a factory, and he was keeping them alive with-- with no touching.
Nobody could touch him.
So he had some kind of a-- a-- going out in Berlin so that nobody knew about it.
And-- and somehow he could take a-- these people and bring them to Brinnlitz.
I never heard about it.
How come I got to Brinnlitz?
Because they didn't have electricians.
So-- and they needed electricians.
They had two electricians up there.
So they needed electricians, so they took from-- Goleszow, you were in--
Not from Goleszow, from-- from Plaszow concentration camp.
They took electricians.
I didn't know that I'm going to Brinnlitz.
I know that I'm going somewheres.
I didn't know where I'm going.
So he knew that some kind of a movement was in Brinnlitz and he didn't care about it.
And when he was leaving, maybe you know the story how he depart from people.
How he made a meeting with the whole factory, with everyone outside.
How he said you're going to be free now, and I have to run because if the Czechs would have got him, they would hang him up-- on the-- on a tree.

So, so what I want to say is, he could have-- he could have smashed the underground group what was in-- in Brinnlitz, but he didn't So and other things what I know was done is, that we, on the end of the war was very hard with food.
So he used to take a truck and go on the front where a horse was killed, he picked them up and brought to the kitchen.
And he said, here's a horse-- do whatever you want, people need to eat something.
He was unbelievable.
The Russians, you were liberated by the Russians?
Then came a day what he left.
He took with him five men whom he chose and they had to be sure that he would not be arrested.
And Schindler's list-- I have it here I'm going to show how this looks.
He took the whole Schindler's list, every name with him.
If a-- whoever would catch him, so he had show that he saved some-- some people.
So he took five or six, I don't remember, five or six people with him.
They should to be sure that he is in good hands.
And he went to Germany, from Czechoslovakia to Germany.
And he got-- got arrested some place [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] near Switzerland.
I don't remember the name.
When the Russians came, first came one Russian on a horse, and he saw that it's a concentration camp.
There was not a factory but he saw that we are in striped uniform.
The date was 5th of-- 5th-- 9th of May, 9th of May.
Matter of fact, we had a woman, we had around 300 women there but around the 300 women was one woman in middle age.
She had two daughters, her name was [NON-ENGLISH], and she kissed this-- this Russian man when he arrived-- the first Russian guy.
Then came army, and they occupied our-- out-- our facilities.
They had the captain of the Russian army, he was the chief of our concentration camp.
And this was the end of the war, what-- what was.
But the-- the-- the four months what I-- it was five months, five months when I was with Schindler-- it was like in a hotel compared with the other one.
After liberation people started to go all places looking for family.
And a man came to our-- out-- our Brinnlitz concentration camp and he said to me, what are you doing here?
Your-- your brother is in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] forgot the name, Czechoslovakia where my wife was.
In Theresienstadt.
So I said, which brother?
He said, your brother Leon is in Czechoslovakia-- I was-- he was with him together.
So I said, OK.
The next day, in the morning, I went to-- to Theresienstadt and I found my brother, and I found my wife there.
My wife was there, also.
When I walked in, there was a-- no, no-- Theresienstadt concentration camp was closed.
You couldn't go in because they had the quarantine.
They had--
Typhus.
So what we did, I cut the wire and went under the wire in the evening, before evening.
I went under the wire and I went in with a friend of mine and walking toward the concentration camp there in Theresienstadt, I found another boy who knew me.
He said, Victor, what are you doing here?
I said, I came to look for my brother.
He said, oh yeah, I saw your brother.
But before I take you to your brother, I'm going to take you some other place where I think you're going to be happy to see it.
And he brought me to my wife.
So this was my meeting with my wife and my brother.
My brother then was 3 60 pounds weight. 3 Now-- now he's 180, but then he was 60 pounds weight.
I took him from there again, in the night-- I took him out through the same hole and I brought him to Brinnlitz.
We had a doctor-- a few doctors in Brinnlitz.
And they told me that they don't know if they can-- they can help him out.
So what I did, I went to Krakow.
I took my brother and we went to Krakow.
Up there was big hospitals.
In Krakow, there were big hospitals.
I went to one of the biggest hospitals and I saw a doctor and the doctor looked on him and-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] looked him up and then he came up out from-- from his room.
And he said, listen, I will tell you one thing-- here in Poland, we cannot help him.
But if you're going to go on American-- where Americans are, there's penicillin, there's all the medicine up there.
You can help--you can save him.
And that's the way how we went back to Ebensee like I told you.
I think I told you that I went out in Ebensee where concentration camp was.
They were occupied by the Americans.
Why did I wind up in Ebensee?
Because the same guy who, no, not the same guy.
I met another guy in Krakow who said, your youngest brother is in Ebensee.
So I took this brother and went to Ebensee.
And in Ebensee, I got to know that my brother is not alive.
But after a few months later, somebody told me that the youngest brother died after the war, a day or two after the war.
I didn't want to see nobody.
Just to look around from outside.
I didn't go to the ghetto.
I didn't go, no.
I was few days there and I was not anxious to look where was ghetto, because everything was occupied by the Polish people.
I know that I cannot go to my apartment where I was born because in this apartment where I was born, I got to know later that there was two families living in this apartment.
In Ebensee I stayed-- from 1945, we went from Ebensee, we went 1946 to Bad Ischl.
And 1946, '47, my daughter was born.
In 1947, we went to Vienna.
In 1947, we went to Vienna.
I was playing soccer in Vienna for Hakoah.
And 1949, we came to the United States.
No, I met my wife in Theresienstadt.
In Krakow.
No, she didn't come with me but she came after me.
She came after me, right.
And we got married in Krakow and after we got married, a day later, we went.
We left and we went to Ebensee.
Right.
In Ebensee, we were a few weeks then we moved to Bad Ischl and from Bad Ischl I was--
And Bad Ischl was a DP camp.
And in this DP Camp, I was chief of police of the DP camp.
The UNRRA proposed me.
Right.
So I said yes.
I was working for them and my father was working for them.
And I had taken the job to be chief of police.
I was handling-- I don't know, you know Austrian people were very angry of those Jews who lived over the war.
Because what happened after the war, we, Jews, got a lot of help from America, and they didn't have what to eat.
Because this was right after the war-- didn't have what to eat.
Until Marshall Plant came.
So they were very-- they were protesting and all kinds of things was going on.
So I had to watch all the camp.
I had police people.
I had 10 policemen and me.
I was the chief and we were watching that everything is going down right.
Matter of fact, there was a--
[SIDE CONVERSATION]
For instance, they used to make [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] protesting.
So they took 500, 600 people and protested against-- against Jews.
Jews have [INAUDIBLE], bread with the signs.
So I used to lock the-- the DP camp.
That our people shouldn't attack them, and they shouldn't attack us.
Because this would be-- it would come to war.
And our-- and our-- at first was around 300 people.
Then we shrink it down to 200 people.
But still 200 people, everybody was not everybody was like me.
I was pretty healthy, but some people were-- my brother, my brother was six months in hospital.
Oh, no, only Jews.
Only Jews, only Jews.
Non-Jews, where did non-Jew.
Come from?
They were not in concentration camps.
No, I know.
Well, there was some.
Oh, so Austrian?
Austrian were Nazis, the biggest Nazis.
This guy what I told you about, the [NON-ENGLISH] who was-- who took me where I got the beating.
He was an Austrian man, and I lock him up after the war.
I went to see him.
He was living in Graz.
I know that he's from Graz.
We look him up, we grab him, lock him up.
I went to America.
I don't know what happened with him.
I was very happy that I saw my wife.
This I knew.
This I knew that they were--
I know that the train went to Belzec This I knew.
And in Belzec-- not one person lived through Belzec.
If I wouldn't have jumped out, I wouldn't be alive either.
Even those people who were burning people, then later they were burned.
Nobody left from Belzec.
If you're going to tell me that somebody-- if you find somebody, somebody is alive who was liberated in Belzec, absolutely not-- absolutely not.
Because Belzec was the camp when they were running out the-- they finished everybody there.
Nobody.
Yeah.
She looked pretty good.
She looked pretty good.
She didn't have hairs, big hairs.
She had the little hair.
She had the handkerchief on her head.
We embraced ourselves.
We kissed ourselves.
And she knew that my brother is very sick.
She knew.
And I asked her to come to Krakow.
I told her, I am taking him to Brinnlitz because I had doctors there, but I'm going to Krakow.
And she had her aunt in together with her.
So I asked her to come to Krakow.
Somehow she delayed coming to Krakow.
I don't know.
We had some disagreement between each other because she wanted to be sure that I am not involved with somebody else.
[LAUGHS] After that issue, you went.
Your brother was recovering in--
My brother recovered in-- in-- in Ebensee.
In Ebensee, right.
Then I left-- no.
I was playing soccer.
And the president of Hakoah in Vienna got to know that somebody of the name Viktor--
Vienna, yes.
Right He created the Hakoah there.
I don't remember his name.
But I will tell you this story, how do I got to Vienna.

So he, the president of Hakoah Vienna, decided-- got to know some boy is in-- is in Bad Ischl and plays soccer for-- I used to play soccer for the Austrians, and I used to play soccer for our Jewish-- Jewish team.
So he came in, and asked me I would like to join his club.
I said, I don't know whatever you're going to offer me, but we'll see.
Second time he came, so he brought his team and he said, your team gonna play my team.
Let's see who's going to play better.
And my wife gave birth to my daughter the same time exactly, the same day.
In Bad Ischl, on Saturday.
So I said, I'm not going to play today because I became a father.
So-- somebody else is going to play on mine.
He said, no, I want to see you playing.
He took a-- a bouquet of roses and went to my wife to hospital, and I went with him.
And he asked her if she would let me play today a game.
She said, yes.
And I played the game.
I hit two goals.
I scored two goals and then he said, I'm going to bring you to Vienna.
But he didn't bring me.
I went myself to him.
I went to play with some of his players to Vienna once but later on I moved along to Vienna.
I was playing soccer and a little bit I had myself on other things.
My friends started businesses there.
So I helped them.
I was working a little bit.
You had an application in, too, for a visa to come to the United States?
I was waiting until I get the visa.
An affidavit.
I had here family who was very, very old man.
He was 92 years old.
I never met him.
He died before I came here but I got a letter from him to Austria, to Vienna, that he's alive.
And when I came to United States I had a hard time.
I couldn't find a job.
My first job was [INAUDIBLE] Matzos.
I was stirring the dough.
I was working there maybe for two months.
They didn't let me work 40 hours, only 30 hours.
They-- they had more people to employ, other people like me.
So they had to spread the hours.
Then I quit the job and I went to learn machine shop.
My brother, who lives here, he is machinist from--
He came eight months before me.
He was a single guy.
He got the visa before I got, and he came here.
He started to work and I came here and started to go to school a little bit to learn English and then to learn some tool making.
And slowly, slowly I went to work and from work-- [INAUDIBLE] And I built myself a business, and I was in business for many years, for around 30 years.
And I will tell you the truth.
God bless America.
America was good to me.
